definition: The sign of stock-bond correlation is determined by the dominant macroeconomic shock type: demand shocks produce negative correlation (central bank eases as equities fall, bonds rally) while supply shocks produce positive correlation (central bank tightens into weakening growth, both fall). The central bank reaction function mediates the transmission.
evidence: Campbell-Pflueger-Viceira (2020) framework endorsed by all eight agents
Across 13 documented post-1960 transitions, 6/7 flips to negative triggered by growth shocks, 5/6 flips to positive by inflation shocks
Median lead time of 2-4 months from shock identification to correlation flip
Current ambiguous shock mix (residual supply-side inflation persistence + demand softening) explains unresolved regime
open_questions: Which shock type will dominate going forward given the mixed signal environment?
How does endogenous fiscal dominance modify the CB reaction function relative to the standard CPV framework?

definition: The current stock-bond correlation regime (Q1 2022-Q1 2026) is transitional and unstable, not settled into persistent positive territory. Rolling 1-year correlation has oscillated between -0.2 and +0.4, reflecting an unresolved conflict between demand-dominant and supply-dominant macro forces.
evidence: Rolling 1-year correlation oscillating between -0.2 and +0.4 since 2022
Eight independent analytical lenses converge on transitional characterization
Four-year duration at upper end of monetary-policy-driven transitions (12-36 months) but within fiscal stalemate transitions (5-7 years)
60/40 demand-dominant vs. supply-dominant probability split estimated by macro_strategist_01
Two of three conjunction conditions met or approaching per rates_strategist_02
open_questions: Is the oscillating regime thesis unfalsifiable as challenger_01 contends?
Will resolution come from a crystallizing event or gradual parametric drift?
How long will the transition persist: 1-3 more years (fiscal stalemate analogue) or less?

definition: Stock-bond correlation is bifurcated by maturity: 2Y Treasury-SPX correlation is negative (reflecting monetary policy expectations) while 30Y Treasury-SPX correlation is mildly positive (+0.05 to +0.15), reflecting term premium pressure from fiscal supply. This pattern is structurally meaningful and reflects partial fiscal dominance.
evidence: Six agents independently document and interpret the pattern across three analytical vocabularies
Expectations vs. term premium decomposition: 2Y approximates expected policy rate, 30Y approximates expected rate plus term premium
Partial fiscal dominance interpretation via Bianchi-Melosi fiscal theory
Pattern survived three iterations of cross-agent scrutiny
Falsifiable prediction: 2Y-SPX turning positive signals full fiscal dominance
open_questions: Is the bifurcation over-fitted to a 3-5 year sample with only 4 historical precedents?
Is +0.05 to +0.15 at the long end statistically distinguishable from zero?
Would the 2Y-SPX turning positive reliably signal full fiscal dominance?

definition: Fiscal dynamics — including deficit-driven Treasury supply ($2T+/year), politically irreversible spending commitments (defense, CHIPS, IRA), and the absence of a credible consolidation path — are a co-equal determinant of the correlation regime alongside monetary policy. The US at ~6.5% deficit/GDP with ~125% debt/GDP is the defining structural feature of this correlation episode.
evidence: Term premium at 50-80bp (ACM), adjusted estimate 80-130bp, driven by $2T+ annual issuance
Defense spending and industrial policy are politically irreversible fiscal commitments
Petrodollar erosion removes price-insensitive Treasury buyers, amplifying fiscal supply pressure
Fiscal trajectory with no consolidation path is the structural difference from prior episodes that reverted to negative correlation
Fiscal dominance makes the CB reaction function endogenous, collapsing the standard Taylor Rule framework
open_questions: At what deficit/GDP threshold does fiscal dominance become full rather than partial?
Can fiscal consolidation occur without a crisis catalyst?
How much of the term premium is attributable to fiscal supply vs. other factors?

definition: Inflation expectations anchoring is the critical state variable determining which correlation regime can prevail. Currently 5Y breakevens sit at 2.3-2.5% (upper bound of historical anchored band 1.8-2.5%), and Michigan 5Y-10Y expectations at 3.0-3.2% are elevated but below the Coibion-Gorodnichenko de-anchoring threshold. The margin of safety is narrow.
evidence: Anchored expectations produce flat Phillips Curve allowing Fed to look through supply shocks producing negative correlation
De-anchored expectations produce steep Phillips Curve forcing Fed to tighten into weakness producing positive correlation
Buffer is thinner than at any point since the late 1990s
A tariff-driven goods inflation shock sustained above 3.5% for 2-3 quarters could breach the de-anchoring threshold
Coibion-Gorodnichenko threshold: sustained >75-100bp divergence for >6 months
open_questions: Are survey-based measures (Michigan) or market-based measures (breakevens) more reliable for identifying de-anchoring?
How quickly can expectations de-anchor given the post-COVID inflation experience?
What is the precise Phillips Curve slope (0.1-0.5) and how does it affect the threshold?

definition: Equities price negative stock-bond correlation (S&P 500 at 21-22x forward P/E requiring bonds-as-insurance) while rates markets and term premium price positive correlation (ACM term premium at +50-80bp). IG credit spreads at post-GFC tights price near-zero recession probability. Gold at ATH prices monetary regime uncertainty. This inconsistency must eventually resolve.
evidence: S&P 500 ERP of ~4.0-4.5% justifiable only under negative correlation assumption
ACM term premium at +50-80bp inconsistent with bonds-as-insurance
Mega-cap tech implies ERP of 2.0-3.0% while S&P 493 implies 5.0-6.5% — index ERP is an averaging artifact
IG credit spreads at ~90-110bp near post-GFC tights
Historical base rate: equity valuations lag correlation regime shifts by 18-36 months
open_questions: Is the equity market mispricing or making an informed forward-looking bet on negative correlation reassertion?
Which market will reprice — equities down or rates/term premium down?
Does passive investing (~50% AUM) extend the repricing lag to 36-60 months?

definition: The S&P 500 ROIC-WACC spread has compressed from ~8-9pp at cyclical peaks to ~3.5-5.0pp currently (tightest since 2007-08), creating nonlinear, convex equity sensitivity to rate changes. At a 4pp spread, 100bp WACC increase destroys ~25% of EVA vs. ~14% at a 7pp spread — a 1.8x amplification. This makes equity earnings approximately 1.5-2.0x more rate-sensitive than in any prior positive correlation episode.
evidence: Mathematical identity: EVA = Invested Capital × (ROIC - WACC)
S&P 500 ROIC declined from 14-15% (2021) to 11-12% (2025E); WACC risen from 6-7% to 8-9%
Current 3.5-5.0pp spread is tightest since 2007-08
In prior positive correlation episodes ROIC-WACC spreads were 8-12pp
Rate increases damage earnings through fundamental channel, not just multiple compression
open_questions: What is the precise current ROIC-WACC spread estimate (±50bp uncertainty)?
How does the compression differ between mega-cap tech (net cash) and S&P 493?
Does AI capex improve ROIC quickly enough to offset WACC pressure?

definition: A formal hierarchy of leading indicators for correlation regime flips, ranked by lead time and reliability: (1) IG credit spreads widening >75bp from trough (18-month lead, 6/7 hit rate), (2) ISM manufacturing <47 for 2+ consecutive months (12-month lead, 5/6), (3) yield curve un-inversion (6-12 months, 4/5), (4) initial claims >300K (3-6 months, 7/8), (5) Fed cumulative cuts >100bp (1-3 months, ~100%). As of March 2026, only Tier 2b (yield curve un-inversion) is active.
evidence: Systematic evidence across 13 post-1960 transitions
Sequencing logic grounded in transmission mechanism: credit markets reprice fastest, then manufacturing, then labor, then Fed responds
Hit rates approximate due to small sample (7 transitions)
Current status: only 1 of 4 tiers active
Probability of correlation flip within 12 months below historical base rate for multi-tier confirmation
open_questions: Are the hit rates stable across different macro regimes or overfitted to post-1960 sample?
Does the current fiscal environment change the lead times?
What is the next critical signal: IG OAS crossing 150bp or ISM sustaining below 47?

definition: $2.5T of corporate debt maturing 2025-2027, with weighted average coupon of ~2.5-3.5% (ZIRP-era issuance) refinancing at 5.5-6.5%, implying ~$50-75B incremental annual interest expense and approximately $2.50-3.75 S&P 500 EPS headwind (~1.0-1.5% of earnings). Impact is concentrated in the S&P 493 (interest expense/EBIT rising from 12% to 22-25% by 2027) while top-10 firms are net cash.
evidence: Rating agency sourced data on maturity profile
Interest expense/EBIT ratio tracking from 12% (2021) to 18% (2025E) to 22-25% (2027) for S&P 493
Top-10 firms interest expense/EBIT only 1-5%
Creates measurable quality divergence reinforcing positive correlation through earnings channel
open_questions: How much will private credit substitution ($1.5T+ AUM) absorb?
Will potential rate cuts materially reduce the refinancing cost?
How does the maturity wall interact with ROIC-WACC compression?

definition: The effective Taylor Rule inflation coefficient (φ_π_eff) has diverged from the stated coefficient to approximately 1.0-1.2, based on revealed-preference estimation from the Fed's 2022-2025 behavior. The Fed paused at 5.25-5.50% with core PCE at 4.3% then cut when core PCE was still 2.7%. At φ_π_eff of 1.0-1.2, the Taylor Principle is barely satisfied, making the correlation sign more shock-dependent than models assuming aggressive central bank credibility would suggest.
evidence: Backward estimation from actual Fed behavior vs. textbook calibrations
Fiscal dominance makes the reaction function endogenous — Fed cannot credibly commit to φ_π > 1.5 when rate hikes blow up deficit via interest on $35T+ debt
Contrasts with textbook 1.5 (Taylor 1993) or 2.0 (Taylor 1999)
Pair_2 judged this stronger than textbook calibration approach
open_questions: What is the precise coefficient estimate (±0.2 uncertainty)?
Has the effective coefficient shifted permanently or cyclically?
Does fiscal dominance structurally prevent the coefficient from returning above 1.5?

definition: Portfolio risk from correlation instability is ~40-50% higher than naive constant-correlation models capture. Moving correlation from -0.30 to +0.30 raises 60/40 portfolio volatility from ~8.4% to ~12.3% (46% increase). Vol-targeting ($500B-$1T AUM) and risk parity ($200-400B AUM) strategies mechanically amplify correlation transitions through forced deleveraging.
evidence: Mechanical calculation: correlation shift from -0.30 to +0.30 produces 46% volatility increase
Vol-targeting and risk parity assume locally stable correlations by construction
Treasury basis trade ($800B-$1T+ at 50-100x leverage) adds acute fragility
Sophisticated institutional models (MSCI Barra, Axioma) do incorporate regime-switching — the claim applies to systematic strategies, not all risk management
open_questions: What fraction of institutional AUM actually uses naive constant-correlation models?
How quickly do vol-targeting strategies deleverage during correlation transitions?
Is the Treasury basis trade at current leverage a systemic risk during correlation transition?

definition: The monetary policy transmission mechanism is partially broken: interest rate channel at ~60% effectiveness (mortgage lock-in), credit channel at ~80%, asset price channel at negative effectiveness (AI rally added $10-15T market cap with wealth effects exceeding rate tightening impulse), exchange rate channel at ~50%, expectations channel at ~50-70%. This explains why financial conditions loosened through 2023-2025 despite the most aggressive hiking cycle since Volcker.
evidence: Goldman FCI eased ~1.0 point despite restrictive rates
AI equity rally loosened conditions through channel orthogonal to monetary policy
Mortgage lock-in effect reduces interest rate channel effectiveness
Forward guidance asymmetrically attenuated: dovish guidance loses ~50% potency under positive correlation
open_questions: Is the transmission breakdown temporary (AI cycle specific) or structural?
What is the precise FCI miscalibration magnitude (40-200bp range)?
Does broken transmission prevent the correlation regime from resolving?

definition: Historically, soft landings have never independently resolved a correlation regime from positive/unstable to durably negative. All five examined episodes required some form of growth shock or financial stress. The directional finding survives despite the statistical weakness of the 0/5 base rate (95% CI extends to ~45% with Laplace correction).
evidence: 0/5 soft-landing episodes resolved correlation without crisis
Market consensus assigns ~40-50% probability to the no-recession, gradual-disinflation, negative-correlation-reassertion scenario
Analogue base rate for this scenario is zero though current environment may be structurally novel
50% passive AUM and AI productivity may create structural novelty
open_questions: Is the current environment structurally different enough to break the historical pattern?
Does 'crisis' need to mean severe recession or could a mild growth scare suffice?
Is n=5 sufficient to draw any inference?

definition: At $2T+ deficit (~6-7% GDP), approximately $400-600B flows annually into corporate profit pools, representing ~25-35% of total S&P 500 net income. The same fiscal expansion sustaining resilient EPS ($230-260 range) is the primary driver of positive long-end correlation via term premium from Treasury supply. Deficit narrowing from 6.5% to 3.5% GDP would mechanically reduce EPS by ~$15-25 but likely restore negative correlation, supporting multiple expansion of 2-4x — a net positive for total returns.
evidence: Accounting identity: fiscal deficit flows into corporate profits after savings offset and net export leakage
~25-35% of S&P 500 net income attributable to deficit channel
Reverse scenario analysis: deficit narrowing trades ~6-10% EPS decline for 2-4x multiple expansion
Equity analysts seeing resilient earnings as bullish ignore that the earnings source compresses the multiple
open_questions: What is the precise savings rate offset and corporate profit share (±30% uncertainty)?
How quickly would multiple expansion offset EPS decline under deficit narrowing?
Does the Kalecki-Levy identity hold at this scale in the current financial system?

definition: Stock-based compensation exclusion from non-GAAP earnings systematically understates equity market effective duration by approximately 25-35%. S&P 500 aggregate SBC of ~$180-200B annually has duration properties (3-5 year vesting, convex option payoff, value scales with multiples) that are implicitly modeled as zero-duration when excluded. In 2022, companies with above-median SBC/revenue underperformed by ~22pp despite negligible non-GAAP impact.
evidence: S&P 500 aggregate SBC of $180-200B annually is a real economic cost
SBC concentrated in terminal value component of equity DCFs
2022 performance divergence: above-median SBC/revenue companies underperformed by ~22pp
Direction near-certain; 25-35% magnitude is constructed estimate without independent academic validation
open_questions: What is the academically validated duration understatement magnitude?
How does SBC duration interact with ROIC-WACC compression?
Should cross-asset risk models recalibrate to GAAP rather than non-GAAP earnings?

definition: A two-dimensional parameter space defined by core inflation (2.0-3.5%) and fiscal deficits (4.5-7.0% GDP) that produces structurally unstable stock-bond correlation. Below the corridor on either variable, one force dominates and correlation resolves. Above on inflation (>4%), positive correlation locks in. Below on deficits (<4% declining), negative correlation reasserts. The US currently occupies this corridor (core PCE ~2.7%, deficit ~6.5% GDP).
evidence: Analytically derived from 4 G10 episodes occupying this parameter space simultaneously
Below corridor on either variable produces stable correlation regime
US occupies corridor: core PCE ~2.7%, deficit ~6.5% GDP
Parameter boundaries carry ±1pp uncertainty from small sample
open_questions: Are the specific boundary parameters (2.0-3.5% inflation, 4.5-7.0% deficit) robust?
How many additional G10 episodes would be needed to validate the boundaries?
Can the corridor framework be extended to non-G10 economies?

definition: Correlation transitions driven by fiscal-monetary stalemate (where fiscal expansion conflicts with monetary tightening) last 5-7 years, roughly 2-3x longer than clean monetary-policy-driven transitions (12-36 months). Three documented analogues: US 1966-71, UK 1972-77, Italy 2008-14. Today's 4-year transition (2022-2026) suggests 1-3 more years absent a crystallizing event.
evidence: US 1966-71 (5 years), UK 1972-77 (5 years), Italy 2008-14 (6 years)
All three cases reflected structural standoff between fiscal expansion and monetary tightening
Current configuration matches: fiscal expansion + monetary tightening
Pair_0 judged this stronger than the 12-36 month monetary-only window
open_questions: Is n=3 sufficient for duration inference?
Does the current fiscal starting point (6-7% deficit, ~125% debt/GDP) exceed all three precedents enough to invalidate the analogue?
Could a crystallizing event shorten the timeline?

definition: USD/JPY carry trade positioning ($500B-$1T estimated) functions as a regime-change accelerator that converts gradual correlation shifts into abrupt flips through forced deleveraging from yen-funded positions hitting both equity and bond markets via margin calls. The July 2024 episode provides out-of-sample confirmation: USD/JPY fell from ~162 to ~142, VIX spiked to 38, and stock-bond correlation temporarily jumped to +0.5.
evidence: July 2024 episode: USD/JPY 162→142, VIX to 38, S&P -5-6%, correlation spike to +0.5
Forced deleveraging mechanism well-understood
Carry positioning estimated at $500B-$1T (not directly observable)
July 2024 positive correlation lasted only days — amplifier vs. cause distinction
open_questions: What is actual carry trade sizing (not directly observable)?
Does BOJ communication under Ueda reduce discontinuity risk vs. Kuroda era?
Is this an amplifier of existing regime shifts or can it independently trigger one?

definition: A Taiwan military contingency would produce a correlation spike to +0.6-0.8 with no Treasury safe-haven rally — the opposite of standard crisis models. Simultaneous semiconductor supply chain disruption (~$2.5T GDP impact), commodity spikes, Chinese UST reserve liquidation ($770B+), sanctions/counter-sanctions, and massive fiscal response would produce a triple loss (equities down, bonds down, dollar uncertain). The scenario implicates the dollar reserve system itself.
evidence: Transmission analysis endorsed as most important tail-risk insight across all analyses
Chinese UST holdings of $770B+ subject to forced liquidation
Semiconductor supply chain disruption impact estimated at ~$2.5T global GDP
Standard Treasury-rally crisis models would fail because the scenario implicates the reserve system
open_questions: What is the event probability (5-15% over 5-year horizon)?
Would Chinese UST liquidation be gradual or sudden?
How would other central banks respond to simultaneous Treasury selling?

definition: The unconditional stock-bond correlation for the next 2-3 years is approximately -0.10 to +0.10, reflecting a mixture distribution of 55-60% Regime D (demand-dominant, correlation -0.30 to -0.50) and 40-45% Regime S (supply-dominant, +0.20 to +0.40). The critical insight is that the distribution is bimodal, not normal, invalidating standard mean-variance optimization using the unconditional near-zero correlation.
evidence: Explicit mixture model: 55-60% Regime D + 40-45% Regime S producing unconditional ~-0.06
Near-zero unconditional robust to ±0.15 perturbations in regime weights
Portfolio optimization using unconditional correlation would be suboptimal due to bimodality
Regime probabilities are calibrated judgment, not model output
open_questions: Can the bimodal distribution be formally tested against a unimodal alternative?
How should portfolio optimization incorporate bimodality?
Are the 55-60%/40-45% regime weights stable or themselves time-varying?

definition: The current macro regime exhibits partial fiscal dominance: the front end of the yield curve still transmits monetary policy intent (2Y-SPX negative correlation) while the back end transmits fiscal supply pressure (30Y-SPX positive correlation). Full fiscal dominance would be signaled by 2Y-SPX turning positive, indicating the central bank has lost control of the short rate's macro transmission.
evidence: Maturity bifurcation documented by six independent agents
Bianchi-Melosi fiscal theory extension of NK model formalizes the mechanism
Fiscal dominance makes the Taylor Rule reaction function endogenous
$2T+ annual issuance with no consolidation path is the structural driver
open_questions: What specific conditions would trigger the transition from partial to full fiscal dominance?
Is 2Y-SPX turning positive a reliable signal or could it reflect other dynamics?
At what debt/GDP ratio does fiscal dominance become irreversible?

definition: Geopolitical escalation produces rapid positive correlation spikes while de-escalation produces slow, partial normalization, creating a ratchet effect that biases expected correlation positive over geopolitical cycles. The mechanism operates through immediate fiscal commitments (defense spending, sanctions compliance, supply chain duplication) that become permanent even after triggering events subside.
evidence: Russia-Ukraine case: European defense spending and energy diversification became structural even as energy prices normalized
Mechanism logically sound: escalation triggers permanent fiscal commitments
Sanctions and export controls create permanent cost-push inflation channels
Small sample of true geopolitical correlation events limits confidence
open_questions: Can the geopolitical channel be isolated from concurrent monetary/fiscal dynamics?
How many geopolitical escalation events are needed to validate the ratchet hypothesis?
Does the ratchet eventually reach a level where it dominates other correlation drivers?

definition: Top 10 stocks (~35% of market cap) are long-duration, rate-sensitive. Index-level stock-bond correlation may primarily reflect mega-cap-tech-bond correlation rather than economy-wide dynamics. Mega-cap tech implies ERP of 2.0-3.0% (pricing permanent negative correlation) while S&P 493 implies 5.0-6.5% (pricing positive correlation). Comparing cap-weighted vs. equal-weight SPX correlation with 10Y yields would resolve this — identified as the single most testable and consequential unresolved hypothesis.
evidence: Top 10 stocks represent ~35% of S&P 500 market cap
Index-level ERP of ~4.5-5.0% is an averaging artifact of two contradictory ERPs
2022 divergence between SBC-heavy and SBC-light companies
Neither challenger_01 nor equity_analyst_02 ran the cap-weighted vs. equal-weight test
open_questions: Does cap-weighted vs. equal-weight SPX correlation with 10Y yields show meaningfully different patterns?
If concentration creates a correlation illusion, does this change the portfolio construction implications?
How does the concentration effect interact with passive investing flows?

definition: Financial conditions indices are miscalibrated by an estimated 75-150bp in policy-rate-equivalent terms due to the AI equity rally loosening conditions through a channel orthogonal to monetary policy. The Fed may be effectively tighter than standard FCI readings suggest, but the specific magnitude is imprecise (plausible range 40-200bp).
evidence: Goldman FCI eased ~1.0 point despite restrictive rates
AI equity rally added $10-15T market cap with wealth effects of $300-750B
Equal-weight vs. cap-weight proxy has its own biases
Direction almost certainly right but magnitude relies on crude proxy
open_questions: What proxy would provide a more precise FCI miscalibration estimate?
Is the miscalibration temporary (AI-specific) or structural?
Should the Fed develop AI-adjusted FCI measures?

definition: BOJ normalization is a potentially underpriced exogenous shock to US term premium and global correlation regimes. Each 25bp BOJ hike narrows the Fed-BOJ spread, reducing carry incentive and strengthening JPY. Estimated Japanese selling pressure of $100-300B over 12-18 months could add 30-180bp to US term premium, but the magnitude range is too wide for confident positioning.
evidence: Each BOJ rate hike narrows Fed-BOJ spread reducing carry incentive
Estimated $100-300B selling pressure over 12-18 months
Japanese institutions gradually reducing UST exposure since 2022 with limited measurable impact
$450B equity ETF position unlikely to be unwound
open_questions: Is the 6x range between low and high estimates actionable?
Has gradual Japanese UST reduction already absorbed much of the potential shock?
How does BOJ communication under Ueda differ from Kuroda in terms of market impact?

definition: The positive stock-bond correlation trade is crowded based on BofA survey data, CFTC positioning, and TLT options skew. This creates contrarian asymmetry favoring negative correlation reassertion if positioning unwinds. The crowding may limit the upside of positive-correlation-positioned portfolios.
evidence: BofA survey data shows consensus positioning for positive correlation
CFTC positioning reflects widespread duration-short positioning
TLT options skew confirms directional crowding
25-30% base rate for recession in any 18-month window with correlation going sharply negative in every recession since 1990
open_questions: How quickly could crowded positioning unwind?
Does the crowding create a self-reinforcing dynamic or a contrarian opportunity?
What is the threshold event that would trigger positioning unwind?
